text: The video shows the ETEP crossover technique, with a clear dissection on the left side before proceeding further.
The surgeon inadvertently enters the peritoneum during dissection, causing the bowel to visibly fall away from the surgical field.
The surgeon performs a quick crossover, developing a retromuscular dissection while maintaining visualization after entering the peritoneal cavity.
The surgeon recruits approximately a quarter of the hernia sac, preparing it for closure of the visceral sac.
The surgeon completes the retromuscular dissection, with partial visualization of the dissection into the space of Retzius, and mobilizes the bladder for optimal utilization.
The surgeon enters the space of Bogros around the arcuate line, developing the space laterally into the retroperitoneum, focusing on posterior tissue advancement.

The surgeon navigates a thin preperitoneal plane, creating multiple holes during the dissection. The peritoneal sac is visible, though its integrity is compromised due to the challenging nature of the dissection.

The surgeon carries the dissection from the bottom up to the subdiaphragmatic region, incising a two-centimeter segment of the transversus abdominis due to multiple holes in the peritoneum. The maneuver is completed with minimal muscle cutting, facilitating a tension-free closure of the visceral sac.

The surgeon completes the closure of the visceral sac, addressing multiple holes created during dissection. The posterior flap is visible, lying loosely and off tension on the underlying viscera, demonstrating robotic suturing techniques.
The surgeon proceeds with anterior fascial closure using a number one permanent V-lock suture on a GS-XXI needle, plicating the diastasis and working towards defect closure.
The surgeon completes the plication of the diastasis and proceeds with the closure of the defect, using sutures to approximate the tissue layers.

The surgeon performs a straightforward closure of the linea alba, noting that the transversus abdominis is not released. The closure technique aims to achieve medialization, simplifying the process despite not releasing the fascia or muscle.
The surgeon places a 30x30 Bard soft mesh into the large retromuscular pocket, trimming the corners for a precise fit during the closure phase.

Due to the significant oozing observed in the surgical field, the surgeon applies Tisseel, a fibrin sealant, and places a retromuscular drain to manage fluid accumulation during the robotic abdominal wall reconstruction.
